source.title: Development and validation of a high density SNP genotyping array for Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar)

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Atlantic salmon lacks a high-density SNP genotyping array, limiting genetic studies and breeding programs. The genome's recent duplication complicates SNP discovery due to paralogous variants. The goal is to develop and validate a dense SNP array using diverse sequencing strategies and rigorous filtering to enable population genetics, linkage mapping, and sex determination.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development and testing of a high-density SNP array for Atlantic salmon. SNP discovery via RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq yielded >400K putative SNPs. An Affymetrix Axiom array was created and tested on 96 samples, resulting in 132,033 polymorphic SNPs with high call rate and good cluster separation. Linkage mapping placed 40,214 SNPs across all 29 chromosomes. IBS clustering distinguished populations, and Y-specific probes accurately predicted sex.
process_signals: Problem: lack of high-density SNP genotyping platform for Atlantic salmon.
Approach: multi-faceted SNP discovery and custom array design.
Key results: 132K polymorphic SNPs, genome-wide coverage, population differentiation, sex prediction.
methods: Array: Affymetrix Axiom myDesign Custom Array
Validation: genotyping 96 samples, linkage mapping, IBS clustering, Y-specific probes

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Atlantic salmon is economically and scientifically important but lacks a high-density SNP array. Existing low-density array (~6K SNPs) and RAD-seq approaches are insufficient for GWAS and genomic selection. The salmonid genome duplication complicates SNP discovery due to paralogous variants. The study aims to develop a high-density array using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq, with strategies to exclude paralogous sequence variants (PSVs).
process_signals: Problem: no high-density SNP array; existing tools insufficient for GWAS/genomic selection.
Constraint: genome duplication leads to PSVs, complicating SNP discovery.
Hypothesis: multi-faceted sequencing and PSV exclusion can yield a dense, reliable SNP array.
methods: PSV exclusion: haploid RR-Seq, Mendelian error filtering
failures_or_limits: Existing array density too low for GWAS.
Genome duplication causes false positive SNPs.

section_gists.heading: Sequencing and SNP discovery
section_gists.gist: Three Illumina-based sequencing strategies were used: RR-Seq (56 fish), RAD-Seq (160 fish), and RNA-Seq (72 fish). Samples included farmed and wild fish, plus a haploid embryo for PSV exclusion. This generated candidate SNPs for the array.
process_signals: Design choice: diverse sequencing strategies to capture SNPs from different genomic regions.
Inclusion of haploid material to identify and exclude PSVs.
methods: Sequencing: Illumina 100 bp PE (and SE for RAD-Seq)

section_gists.heading: SNP selection and filtering
section_gists.gist: Alignment to draft genome identified 472K (RR-Seq), 467K (RAD-Seq), and 816K (RNA-Seq) putative SNPs. After quality filtering, 99K, 83K, and 229K remained. Additional public SNPs were included. All 411K candidates were submitted to Affymetrix for p-convert scoring. Final array contained 286K putative SNPs assayed by 443K probes.
process_signals: Filtering step: quality control reduced putative SNPs significantly.
Design choice: used p-convert scores to select SNPs likely to convert to reliable assays.
Inclusion of public domain SNPs to supplement discovery.
failures_or_limits: Large reduction from putative to candidate SNPs, especially RNA-Seq (816K to 229K).

section_gists.heading: Performance of SNPs on the array
section_gists.gist: Genotyping 96 diverse samples yielded 135,682 high-quality polymorphic SNPs. Main reason for discarding: monomorphism (110,910 SNPs). After Mendelian error filtering, final set: 132,033 SNPs. RNA-Seq had highest dropout (68%), RAD-Seq 50%, RR-Seq 32%. Duplicated genome caused multi-site variants (MSVs) complicating clustering. Previously published SNPs had 23% non-verification.
process_signals: Observation: high monomorphism rate, especially in RNA-Seq SNPs.
Failure: RNA-Seq SNPs had high false positive rate; RAD-Seq also high dropout.
Constraint: genome duplication led to MSVs, requiring special clustering.
Validation: Mendelian error filtering removed 3,649 SNPs.
Partial result: 23% of previously published SNPs not verified.
methods: Genotyping: Affymetrix Axiom array on 96 samples
Quality categories: polymorphic high resolution, monomorphic, low call rate, etc.
Clustering: Axiom GT1 algorithm, handling MSVs
Mendelian error check using pedigreed samples
failures_or_limits: RAD-Seq dropout higher than RR-Seq, possibly due to less effective PSV removal.

section_gists.heading: Population segregation of SNPs
section_gists.gist: Filtered SNPs were highly polymorphic across farmed Scottish, farmed Norwegian, and wild populations. Over 90K SNPs had MAF>0.05 in all groups. Farmed Scottish had most population-specific SNPs (~8K), likely due to ascertainment bias. Average MAF overall was 0.25.
process_signals: Validation: SNPs segregate in diverse populations.
Observation: ascertainment bias towards farmed Scottish population.
Metric: >90K SNPs shared across groups with MAF>0.05.
methods: MAF calculation in unrelated individuals per population
failures_or_limits: Ascertainment bias due to discovery panel composition.

section_gists.heading: Genomic distribution of SNPs
section_gists.gist: SNPs were distributed across ~71K contigs, with 59% containing only one SNP. Average spacing ~18 kb genome-wide, ~6 kb on SNP-containing contigs. Linkage mapping using lack of male recombination placed 40,214 SNPs across all 29 chromosomes. SNP count per chromosome correlated with previous map (r=0.84).
process_signals: Validation: genome-wide coverage confirmed by contig distribution and linkage mapping.
Method: exploited male recombination suppression for mapping.
Result: 92% of sire-segregating SNPs mapped.
methods: Linkage mapping: CriMap v2.4, using SalMap families Br5 and Br6
Correlation with Lien et al. map
failures_or_limits: Some chromosomes underestimated due to mapping limitations.

section_gists.heading: Identity-by-state clustering and multidimensional scaling
section_gists.gist: IBS pairwise distances and MDS clearly separated farmed Scottish, two farmed Norwegian populations, and wild fish. Demonstrates array's utility for population structure analysis.
process_signals: Validation: array can distinguish populations.
Method: IBS and MDS using Plink.
failures_or_limits: Small sample sizes per population; further validation needed.

section_gists.heading: Predicting phenotypic sex using Y-specific probes
section_gists.gist: 87 Y-specific probes based on sdY gene were included. Mean intensity values clustered samples into male/female groups. 100% concordance with known phenotypic sex in 63 samples. Provides molecular sexing tool.
process_signals: Design choice: included Y-specific probes for sex determination.
Validation: 100% accuracy in tested samples.
methods: Probe design from Atlantic salmon sdY sequence

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: First high-density (~130K) SNP array for Atlantic salmon. Three discovery techniques successful. SNPs widely distributed. Array will be publicly available for genetic studies and genomic selection.
process_signals: Achievement: successful array development and validation.
Future: public availability, applications in research and breeding.

section_gists.heading: Affymetrix Axiom array creation and genotyping
section_gists.gist: Candidate SNPs submitted as 71-mers with alleles at position 36. p-convert scores computed. Probes designed in forward/reverse; recommended and neutral probes included. 266,105 SNPs tiled (93% capacity). Remainder filled with multi-discovery, low p-convert, and previously verified SNPs. Most SNPs have two probesets. Genotyping on 96 samples: farmed Scottish, Norwegian, wild. Axiom GT1 algorithm for clustering. SNPs categorized by QC: polymorphic high resolution, monomorphic, etc. Only categories (i) and (iv) used. OTVs identified and handled. Final data analyzed with Plink.
process_signals: Design choice: p-convert threshold for probe selection.
Inclusion strategy: recommended/neutral probes, then fill with lower confidence SNPs.
Genotyping QC: multiple categories, strict filtering.
Handling of OTVs due to paralogous variants.
methods: QC categories: polymorphic high resolution, monomorphic, low call rate, no minor homozygote, OTV, other
Plink for Mendelian error and analysis
failures_or_limits: OTVs indicate presence of paralogous variants interfering with genotyping.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: A high-density SNP array can be developed for Atlantic salmon despite genome duplication by using multiple sequencing strategies and stringent filtering for paralogous variants.
The array will enable population genetics studies, linkage mapping, and sex determination.
RNA-Seq will contribute many SNPs but may have higher false positive rates.
Inclusion of haploid material and Mendelian error checking will reduce PSVs.
The array will show ascertainment bias towards the discovery populations.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact criteria for initial quality-control filtering of putative SNPs (not detailed in these sections).
Specific p-convert thresholds used for inclusion.
Details of PSV exclusion methods beyond haploid RR-Seq and Mendelian errors.
Performance of the array in additional populations not tested.
Long-term utility for GWAS and genomic selection not yet demonstrated.

events.summary: Lack of high-density SNP genotyping platform for Atlantic salmon hampers genetic studies and breeding programs.
A multi-faceted SNP discovery approach using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq with PSV exclusion can yield a dense, reliable SNP array.
SNP discovery performed using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq on farmed and wild samples, including haploid embryo for PSV exclusion.
Alignment to draft genome identified >400K putative SNPs; quality-control filtering reduced candidates for array design.
methods_or_tools: draft Atlantic salmon reference genome assembly (GCA_000233375.1)

events.summary: Affymetrix Axiom myDesign Custom Array created with 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes, using p-convert scores for selection.
Array tested on 96 diverse samples; 135,682 SNPs designated high quality polymorphic, but 110,910 monomorphic.
Mendelian error filtering removed 3,649 SNPs, resulting in final set of 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs.
High dropout rates: RNA-Seq 68%, RAD-Seq 50%, RR-Seq 32%; RNA-Seq particularly susceptible to false positives.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, background, and results sections detailing problem, hypothesis, design, computation, experiment, analysis, and failure events.

events.summary: Evaluated segregation of filtered SNPs in distinct Atlantic salmon populations, finding high polymorphism and ascertainment bias.
Investigated genomic distribution of SNPs across reference genome contigs, finding wide coverage and average spacing.
Validated chromosome assignment by correlating SNP counts per chromosome with previous linkage map (r=0.84).
Noted small sample sizes for population structure analysis, suggesting further validation needed.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers population segregation, genomic distribution, linkage mapping, IBS clustering, sex prediction, and methods for haploid creation and sequencing library preparation. Events focus on analysis and validation of the SNP array.

events.summary: RAD library preparation and sequencing for SNP discovery
RNA-Seq library preparation and sequencing for SNP discovery
methods_or_tools: Illumina Truseq RNA Sample Preparation kit v1

chunk_events.notes: This chunk describes the detailed experimental protocols for RAD-Seq and RNA-Seq library preparation and sequencing, as well as the computational filtering steps for RR-Seq SNP discovery, including PSV exclusion. These are core methods for the array development.

events.summary: RR-Seq reads aligned to reference genome using BWA, and SNPs filtered by haploid genotype, allele frequency, depth, and repeats.

entities: Atlantic salmon genome assembly (NCBI Assembly GCA_000233375.1)
numbers_or_metrics: perfect end-to-end and gapless alignment of seed substrings of 32-mers

events.summary: Affymetrix Axiom array created with 266,105 SNPs tiled using p-convert scores and probe recommendations.
Genotyping of 96 samples performed, SNPs categorized by clustering performance.
numbers_or_metrics: categories: (i) polymorphic high resolution, (ii) monomorphic high resolution, (iii) call rate below threshold, (iv) no minor homozygote, (v) off-target variant, (vi) other

events.summary: Linkage analysis performed using sire-based inheritance to map SNPs to chromosomes.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for SNP discovery filtering, array design, genotyping, linkage mapping, and population analysis, all directly supporting the paper's process.
This chunk contains only the reference list of the paper. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: In Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar), these goals are currently hampered by the lack of a high-density SNP genotyping platform.

a multi-faceted approach to SNP discovery was applied using a combination of RR-Seq, RAD-Seq and RNA-Seq alongside several strategies for exclusion of paralogous sequence variants (PSV) including RR-Seq of haploid material.
three main Illumina – based sequencing strategies were applied; RR-Seq (56 fish), RAD-Seq (160 fish) and RNA-Seq (72 fish).
the RR-Seq also included a pool of wild fish from diverse geographical sources and a single haploid Atlantic salmon embryo for the purpose of PSV exclusion
Following the quality-control filtering of these putative SNPs (described in 'Methods'), 99,097 (RR-Seq), 83,151 (RAD-Seq) and 229,754 (RNA-Seq) candidate SNPs remained
the final array contained 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes.
The ssalar01 array was used to genotype 96 Atlantic salmon samples of diverse origin

a total of 135,682 SNPs were designated as high quality and polymorphic. The main reason for discarding SNPs at this stage was the high rate of monomorphism, with 110,910 SNPs designated as monomorphic

Following a final filtering stage based on exclusion of any genotyped SNPs showing an apparent Mendelian error in the pedigreed samples, the final set of 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs were used for further analysis
the majority of candidate SNPs provided to Affymetrix were derived from RNA-Seq, reflecting the high discrepancy between candidate and verified RNA-Seq-derived SNPs (68% drop out).
The segregation of the filtered SNPs in the samples from distinct populations of Atlantic salmon was evaluated.
The distribution of the ssalar01 array QC-filtered SNPs across the reference genome contigs was investigated.
Approximately 71 K contigs contained one or more SNPs with the majority of those (59%) containing only one SNP.
The number of SNPs per chromosome showed a high positive correlation (r = 0.84, Figure 3B) with the SNP linkage map of Lien et al.
These results are indicative of the validity of the chromosome assignment for the SNPs, and suggestive of their genome-wide distribution.

The number of fish included per population was relatively small, particularly for the wild samples, and genotyping additional unrelated samples from each of these (and other) populations would be advantageous to fully evaluate the utility of the array to detect population structure.
The RAD library preparation protocol employed in this study has been fully documented elsewhere [49].
Each sample (1.5 μg parental DNA or 0.5 μg offspring DNA) was digested at 37°C for 45 minutes with Sbfl high fidelity restriction enzyme
individual specific P1 adapters, each with a unique 5 base barcode were ligated to the Sbf1 digested DNA
run on an Illumina Hiseq 2000
sequenced in barcoded pools of 12 individual fish on the Illumina Hiseq 2000 instrument (100 base paired-end sequencing, v3 chemistry)
Of the putative SNPs remaining, those with an allele frequency of ≤0.1 or a read depth of ≤10 (n = 172,501) were removed.
Duplicated reads originating from PCR were marked using Picard and subsequently ignored.
SNPs that demonstrated two or more mendelian errors or significant mendelian distortion (chi2 P <0.05) in any of the families (n = 344,278) were removed.
The remainder were repeat-masked as above (39,839 removed) leaving 83,151 candidate RAD-Seq-derived SNPs.
Only the 426,135 transversions (which are best-suited for inclusion on the Axiom array) with a predicted MAF≥0.1 were retained.
These were repeat-masked as above (196,381 removed) which left 229,754 candidate RNA-Seq-derived SNPs.
Using PCR primer sets designed from both these sequences, partial sequences from the SRY gene in Atlantic salmon were obtained by direct sequencing of amplicons.
The candidate SNPs were provided to Affymetrix as 71-mer nucleotide sequences from the forward strand with the alleles at the target SNP highlighted at position 36.

All 'recommended' probes were included and 'neutral' probes were included if paired with a 'recommended' or a 'neutral' probe, resulting in the tiling of probes for 266,105 putative SNPs, this being 93% of the capacity of the array.
In the final array, most of the SNPs are interrogated by two independent probesets, designed at the 5' and at the 3' of the SNP.

The SNPs were split into categories according to their clustering performance with respect to various Axiom-generated quality-control criteria; (i) ‘polymorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC, (ii) ‘monomorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC except the presence of a minor allele in two or more samples, (iii) ‘call rate below threshold’ where genotype call rate is under 97%, (iv) ‘no minor homozygote’ where the SNP passes all QC but only two clusters are observed, (v) ‘off-target variant’ (OTV) where atypical cluster properties arise from variants in the SNP flanking region, and (vi) ‘other’ where the SNP does not fall into any of the previous categories.
In this study, only SNPs from categories (i) and (iv) were included in further analyses.

This analysis relied on the lack of male recombination in centromeric regions of the male salmonid genome, and this feature facilitated mapping of markers to linkage groups according to identical or near-identical sire-based inheritance patterns.

Libraries were checked for quality and quantified using the Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent, USA), before being sequenced in barcoded pools of 12 individual fish on the Illumina Hiseq 2000 instrument (100 base paired-end sequencing, v3 chemistry) and all sequence data were deposited in the ENA under accession number ERP003968.
A total of 150 $\mu$ L of each amplified library (14 - 16 PCR cycles) was size selected (c.

Shearing (Covaris S2 sonication) and initial size selection (250 – 500 bp) by agarose gel separation was followed by gel purification, end repair, dA overhang addition, P2 paired-end adapter ligation, library amplification, as in the original RAD protocol [46].

Libraries were quantified using the Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent, USA), library-specific nucleotide barcodes were added, and they were sequenced in multiplexed pools on the Illumina Hiseq 2000 instrument using a 100 base paired-end sequencing strategy (v3 chemistry).

Secondly, a 'two-point' linkage analysis was performed to determine the LOD scores between all pairs of markers in randomly selected pools of \~5,000 SNPs including anchor markers from each of the 29 pairs of Atlantic salmon chromosomes (Additional file 1: Table S2).
RNA-Seq libraries each comprised of six individual homogenised whole fry (each $\sim$ 0.5 g) per family per timepoint (total n=72).
Genomic DNA fragments of between 450-550 bp were size-selected by agarose gel electrophoresis and the gel slices were purified using a MinElute Gel Extraction kit (Qiagen, UK).
For libraries 1 - 5, equal amounts of individual genomic DNA was multiplexed to form pools of total 15 $\mu$ g and, for library 6.5 $\mu$ g genomic DNA from the haploid sample was used.

All candidate SNPs (total of 411,308; 4,139 of which were detected in more than one SNP discovery category) were submitted to Affymetrix for in silico prediction of their probability of conversion to a reliable assay on the Axiom array (p-convert score).

Results: SNP discovery was performed using extensive deep sequencing of Reduced Representation (RR-Seq), Restriction site-Associated DNA (RAD-Seq) and mRNA (RNA-Seq) libraries derived from farmed and wild Atlantic salmon samples (n = 283) resulting in the discovery of > 400 K putative SNPs.

gh resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC except the presence of a minor allele in two or more samples, (iii) ‘call rate below threshold’ where genotype call rate is under 97%, (iv) ‘no minor homozygote’ where the SNP passes all QC but only two clusters are observed, (v) ‘off-target variant’ (OTV) where atypical cluster properties arise from variants in the SNP flanking region, and (vi) ‘other’ where the SNP does not fall into any of the previous categories. OTVs are reproducible and previously uncharacterized var
) Libraries were accurately quantified by qPCR (Kapa Library), combined as appropriate and run on an Illumina Hiseq 2000.
Following heat inactivation individual ligation reactions were then combined in appropriate multiplex pools / libraries (5 parental samples or 15 offspring samples each).
Equimolar amounts of all four libraries were combined and run on a single Illumina Hiseq 2000 lane, giving three-fold deeper coverage of parental samples cf.
For each year-group, four RAD libraries were constructed; two parental libraries (five individuals each) and two offspring libraries (15 individuals each).

RAD sequencing was undertaken for five randomly selected family groups (both parents and six offspring; n = 40 individuals) from each of the four year-group subpopulations that comprise the LNS broodstock population.
For libraries 1 - 5, equal amounts of individual genomic DNA was multiplexed to form pools of total 15 $\mu$ g and, for library 6.
Library 5 comprised a pool of genomic DNA from 16 wild fish (sex unknown) with four from each of four populations sources in Scotland, Norway, Ireland and Spain, respectively.

Finally, it is noteworthy that in the final set of QC-filtered SNPs, at least 38% (the RNA-Seq-derived SNPs) are from transcribed regions of Atlantic salmon genome (Figure 1) and therefore more likely to be functional, and this putative enrichment is advantageous for determining the genetic architecture of traits of economic or environmental importance and for comparative mapping between salmonids and more distantly-related species.
Probes designed to detect SNP alleles in a single genome location can often also detect paralogous alleles which gives rise to multi-site variants (MSV; [36]).
At least 38% of these SNPs are from transcribed genomic regions and therefore more likely to include functional variants.
Sanchez CC, Smith TP, Wiedmann RT, Vallejo RL, Salem M, Yao J, 3rd Rexroad CE: Single nucleotide polymorphism discovery in rainbow trout by deep sequencing of a reduced representation library.
Library 6 comprised a single haploid fish and was sequenced for the purpose of identification and exclusion of PSV.
Libraries 1 - 4 each corresponded to a pool of genomic DNA of ten fish (five male, five female) from each of the four year-group subpopulations of the LNS broodstock population.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Atlantic salmon lacks a high-density SNP genotyping array, limiting genetic studies and breeding programs. The genome's recent duplication complicates SNP discovery due to paralogous variants.

canonical_events.summary: Lack of high-density SNP genotyping platform for Atlantic salmon hampers genetic studies and breeding programs.
A multi-faceted SNP discovery approach using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq with PSV exclusion can yield a dense, reliable SNP array.
SNP discovery performed using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq on farmed and wild samples, including haploid embryo for PSV exclusion.
Alignment to draft genome identified >400K putative SNPs; quality-control filtering reduced candidates for array design.
key_entities: draft Atlantic salmon reference genome assembly (GCA_000233375.1)

canonical_events.summary: Affymetrix Axiom myDesign Custom Array created with 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes, using p-convert scores for selection.
Array tested on 96 diverse samples; 135,682 SNPs designated high quality polymorphic, but 110,910 monomorphic.
Mendelian error filtering removed 3,649 SNPs, resulting in final set of 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs.
High dropout rates: RNA-Seq 68%, RAD-Seq 50%, RR-Seq 32%; RNA-Seq particularly susceptible to false positives.
Evaluated segregation of filtered SNPs in distinct Atlantic salmon populations, finding high polymorphism and ascertainment bias.
Investigated genomic distribution of SNPs across reference genome contigs, finding wide coverage and average spacing.
Linkage mapping placed 40,214 SNPs across all 29 chromosomes, exploiting lack of male recombination.
Validated chromosome assignment by correlating SNP counts per chromosome with previous linkage map (r=0.84).
Y-specific probes based on sdY gene accurately predicted phenotypic sex with 100% concordance in 63 samples.
Noted small sample sizes for population structure analysis, suggesting further validation needed.

edges.reason: Sequencing data enables SNP discovery and filtering.
Filtered candidate SNPs are used to design the array.
Genotyping results are filtered to obtain final SNP set.
High dropout rates reveal failure of some SNP discovery methods.
Final SNP set is used for population segregation analysis.
Final SNP set is used for genomic distribution analysis.
Final SNP set is used for linkage mapping.
Linkage mapping results are validated by correlation with previous map.
Array design includes Y-specific probes for sex determination.

must_cover_points: Problem: lack of high-density SNP array for Atlantic salmon.
Hypothesis: multi-faceted SNP discovery with PSV exclusion.
Design: RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, RNA-Seq with haploid embryo.
Analysis: Mendelian error filtering to final SNP set.
Failure: high dropout rates, especially RNA-Seq.
Analysis: population segregation and ascertainment bias.
Validation: linkage mapping and correlation with previous map.
Validation: sex prediction with Y-specific probes.
Limitation: small sample sizes for population analysis.
